Empty. I have enough domain knowledge. Let me reason and finalize.

**Analysis:**

The current best (NMSE_val 0.0597) is a symmetric logistic with concentration-dependent rate, power-law half-time, and affine seed shift. The most physically-motivated and structurally-cheap improvement is to break the symmetry constraint.

Amyloid sigmoid curves (ThT fluorescence) are typically **asymmetric**: a slow nucleation-dominated lag followed by rapid elongation, with the approach to plateau being sharper or softer than the initial rise. A symmetric logistic forces both halves to mirror, which is a structural bias causing residual NMSE.

The **generalized logistic (Richards) curve** adds a single shape exponent `nu`:

    y = plateau / (1 + exp(-rate*(x0 - half_time)))^nu + baseline

This captures asymmetry with exactly one extra constant. It stays smooth and finite everywhere (the denominator base `1+exp(...)` is always ≥ 1, so raising it to any power `nu` is well-defined and positive — no singularities or non-real values). When `nu = 1` it reduces exactly to the proven logistic, so the optimizer can only improve.

One numerical caution: `nu` must keep the base positive — it always is here since `1+exp(z) > 1`. I'll initialize `nu = 1.0` so the fit starts at the known-good logistic and refines from there.

This is the smallest change that targets the most likely limitation (shape misfit), adds one constant (total 8, well under 13), and keeps complexity modest.
